source: Temperley-Kostka-Payne (CBMS) key profiles: Temperley, Music and Probability (MIT Press, 2007), derived from the Kostka-Payne theory-textbook corpus. Vectors verified digit-for-digit against music21 analysis/discrete.py (TemperleyKostkaPayneKeyWeights) and Sapp's Humdrum keycor. Documented biases (use as an OPT-IN alternative, validate on the target corpus): well-balanced for MAJOR keys (corrects the Krumhansl-Kessler dominant bias); but a known tendency to read the RELATIVE MAJOR as tonic in minor keys. Added A6 brief-9/research-9: on the SWD residual it recovers the dominant-substitution (e.g. A maj read as E maj) and parallel near-tie cases under the engine's Pearson scoring, trading kk-1982.1's dominant bias for a relative-major bias. THIS IS THE DEFAULT as of 2026-06-17 (A6 brief-10): validated full-corpus on SWD-24 across all three key surfaces (global +12.5pp, windowed +15.5pp, structural +13pp; brief-11/12), a coordinated change to the A5/A7 stability contract — those consumers pin profile_version='kk-1982.1' to retain the old margins, because CBMS is mode-asymmetric on the MARGIN scale (major margins inflate, minor compress) and so suits ranking-accuracy consumers (A6/A1) over margin-as-control-signal consumers (A5/A7). The relative-major-in-minor bias above is the residual: D911-03/08/07/16 are its acceptance set for the deferred mode-aware/closure induction lever.
Krumhansl & Kessler (1982) probe-tone profiles, as tabulated in Krumhansl, Cognitive Foundations of Musical Pitch (1990)
